items.q: Why are cash flow and profit different on different time-scales?
items.correct: Profit is recognised when a sale is made (even on credit); cash flow is when money actually moves. A profitable sale today may produce no cash for 60 days while bills are due — creating a cash-flow problem despite profit.
items.misconception: Cash flow and profit are the same thing measured differently.
items.random_wrong: Cash flow only matters at year-end.
items.why: The timing gap between recognition and receipt is what sinks profitable companies. Students conflate the two accounting concepts, missing why working capital management is critical.

items.q: Which sector does manufacturing belong to?
items.correct: The secondary sector — which transforms raw materials from the primary sector (farming, mining) into finished goods.
items.misconception: Manufacturing is in the primary sector because it makes things.
items.random_wrong: Manufacturing is in the tertiary sector along with services.
items.why: Primary = extracting raw materials; secondary = transforming them; tertiary = services. 'Making things' suggests primary to students, but only extraction is primary — building/processing is secondary.

items.q: What did Mayo's Hawthorne studies suggest about motivation?
items.correct: Workers are motivated by social factors and the feeling of being noticed/valued — not just pay or physical conditions. Productivity rose because researchers paid attention to workers, not because lighting improved.
items.misconception: Mayo proved that better lighting increases productivity.
items.why: The Hawthorne effect is famously about attention and social meaning, not the experimental variable itself. Students remember the lighting experiment and miss the human relations conclusion Mayo drew from it.

items.q: Which is NOT one of the 4 Ps?
items.why: It's Profit. “Product” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The answer examiners want is: Unique Selling Point. “United Sales Plan” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A T-shirt costs £4 to make and sells for £10. Profit per shirt, in £?
items.why: The real figure is 6. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
